root_text.an5.96:1.1: “Pañcahi, bhikkhave, dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu ānāpānassatiṁ āsevanto nacirasseva akuppaṁ paṭivijjhati.
root_text.an5.96:1.3: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu appaṭṭho hoti appakicco subharo susantoso jīvitaparikkhāresu;
root_text.an5.96:1.6: bahussuto hoti sutadharo sutasannicayo, ye te dhammā ādikalyāṇā majjhekalyāṇā pariyosānakalyāṇā sātthaṁ sabyañjanaṁ kevalaparipuṇṇaṁ parisuddhaṁ brahmacariyaṁ abhivadanti, tathārūpāssa dhammā bahussutā honti dhātā vacasā paricitā manasānupekkhitā diṭṭhiyā suppaṭividdhā;
root_text.an5.96:1.8: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu ānāpānassatiṁ āsevanto nacirasseva akuppaṁ paṭivijjhatī”ti.

translation_text.an5.96:1.1: “Mendicants, a mendicant cultivating mindfulness of breathing who has five things will soon penetrate the unshakable.
translation_text.an5.96:1.3: It’s when a mendicant has few obligations and duties, and is unburdensome and contented with life’s necessities.
translation_text.an5.96:1.4: They eat little, not devoted to filling their stomach.
translation_text.an5.96:1.5: They are rarely drowsy, and are dedicated to wakefulness.
translation_text.an5.96:1.6: They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically.
translation_text.an5.96:1.7: They review the extent of their mind’s freedom.
translation_text.an5.96:1.8: A mendicant cultivating mindfulness of breathing who has these five things will soon penetrate the unshakable.”
